text: a patient of fifty-two years old with a tumor of two and a half centimeters in the head of the pancreas.
The robotic surgical system is positioned with its target set on the region of the biliary vesicle, approximately twenty centimeters away from the pylorus transition.
The robotic XI platform is utilized in the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure, providing enhanced precision and control during the surgical intervention.
The surgeon opens the gastrocolic space and releases the pancreaticoduodenal block, mobilizing it medially using the back maneuver.
The surgeon carefully dissects the duodenum to access the retroperitoneal space, revealing the lower cava vein.
The surgeon continues the dissection cranially from the identified lower cava vein, progressing towards the left renal vein, using robotic assistance.
which is our upper limit of dissection in dodenoparatectomy commonly used by our service.
After identifying the left renal vein, the surgeon performs a lateral dissection using the robotic XI platform, carefully separating the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon visualizes the upper mesenteric artery and the aorta, following the medial mobilization of the pancreaticoduodenal block.
The robotic calipers demonstrate a high degree of mobility and freedom, while the robotic hook efficiently dissipates energy, facilitating precise dissection.
The surgeon performs careful hemostasis control to maintain a dry operating field, ensuring clear visibility of the anatomical structures during the pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon opens the tissue in the posterior direction towards the spine, revealing the aorta artery.
The surgeon performs dissection on the anterior face of the aortic intercave space, followed by a wide and careful lymphadenectomy in this region.
The surgeon mobilizes and dries small lymph nodes, including retrocaval nodes, using surgical instruments within the operating field.
The assistant uses a suction device to extract smoke generated by the dissection instruments. Following the peritoneal lymphadenectomy, the surgical team proceeds to the hepatic islet.

The surgeon elevates the liver using an auxiliary clamp connected to the diaphragm, while another clamp applies traction to the visceral biliary structures. A lymphadenectomy is performed on the hepatic islet, common hepatic artery, and surrounding lymph nodes.

The degree of mobility of robotic tweezers is much higher than that of laparoscopic tweezers. Therefore, we were able to perform a minimally invasive surgery within the same standard of a laparotomic surgery.
The surgical team uses a hook instrument to perform lymphadenectomy, leveraging its high degree of freedom for precise dissection of lymph nodes.

The surgeon completes a 360-degree dissection around the hepatic artery, followed by lymphadenectomy of the pericoledocyan and periportal structures. The gastrodendron artery is dissected and sealed with plastic clips. The hepatic artery, portal vein, and main biliary pathways are clearly visible.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the hepatic gland, progressing towards the hepatic duct. The collar is visibly pulled and mobilized, while connective and periportal tissues are dissected.

Following the dissection of the hepatic duct, the surgeon temporarily occludes it with a bulldog clamp. The procedure then moves to the gastric region, where a gastrotomy is performed using laparoscopic linear staplers.
The surgeon exposes the anterior face of the pancreas and releases both the lower and upper edges. The back angle is mobilized, followed by an enterotomy and careful mobilization of the duodenum.

The surgeon mobilizes the fourth portion of the duodenum until it meets the third portion, carefully manipulating the pancreas to ensure clear visibility of the entire pancreatic circumference for future anastomosis.

The surgeon identifies the splenic vein in the retro-pancreatic position and performs dissection of the superior mesenteric vein. Using robotic dissection, the first regional branch is clipped, and the Whipple maneuver is executed. The junction of the superior mesenteric vein with the splenic vein forming the portal vein is clearly visible.
The surgeon uses a harmonic bisturi to perform a pancreatotomy, carefully cutting through the pancreatic tissue until the pancreatic duct is identified.

The surgeon coldly sections the pancreatic duct using scissors, while the remaining pancreatic tissue is sectioned with a harmonic device. The view reveals the pancreatic cut edge, along with the superior mesenteric vein and the splenic vein.

The surgeon progressively mobilizes the uncinate process with careful maneuvers, removing it along with the surrounding tissue. Traction is applied to the duodenum and proximal jejunum, positioning the resected tissue on the right flank of the patient.

The surgeon carefully dissects the vasculature around the sinus process, using clamps to achieve the final release of the operating part. The surgical field provides a general view of the dissection, including the left renal vein, aorta, hepatic artery, and superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon removes the biliary vesicle and identifies the regional bone, initiating the reconstruction of the digestive tract following the resection.

The surgeon performs the pancreatic anastomosis using a splint to identify the pancreatic duct, facilitating the anastomosis. A posterior plan is created with continuous suture using prolene, followed by the mucosal duct anastomosis. The jejunal pancreatic anastomosis is completed using a robotic platform, providing enhanced visualization and precise movement of the instruments. The jejunal handle is opened with a small incision, and sutures are placed between the pancreatic duct and the jejunal mucosa.

The surgeon performs the mucosal duct anastomosis using separate sutures of PDS 5-0. After completing the posterior wall of the anastomosis, a trans-anastomotic splint is introduced to facilitate the procedure, followed by the completion of the mucosal duct anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the pancreatic-jejunal anastomosis using polypropylene sutures to ensure secure closure between the pancreas and jejunum. The round ligament, previously dissected and released, is utilized to create a reinforcing patch around the anastomosis site.

Following the completion of the pancreatic-jejunal anastomosis, the surgeon begins the hepatic-jejunal anastomosis using a continuous suture technique with the invaginative structure of Schmieden. The posterior wall is sutured first, followed by the anterior wall to complete the anastomosis. The surgeon then identifies the fast and performs a gastroenterostomy at a point forty centimeters from the hepatic anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a trans-anastomotic nascent probe for early feeding following gastro-duodenal pancreatectomy. A mechanical gastroenterone anastomosis is performed with linear anastomosis, closing gaps in two planes. An entero-entero-anastomosis is systematically executed to minimize biliary reflux and reduce the possibility of afferent sinus.

The surgeon carefully reviews hemostasis, placing reinforcement sutures at the anastomoses. A blade drain number twenty-four is positioned for drainage, and the surgical specimen is removed through an incision.
